title: TopologicalOrder
tex_block_html: Let $G=(J,V,E,L)$ be a CDMG. A topological order of $G$ is a total order $<$ of $J \cup V$ such that for all $v,w \in G$: \[ v \in \Pa^G(w) \; \implies \; v < w.\] Equivalently, it can be described as an indexing of the nodes $J \cup V = \{v_1,\dots,v_K\}$ where parents always precede their children.

tex_statement.raw:
% Definition file for row def_3_8 -- "TopologicalOrder".
% Auto-generated stub by scaffold/solve_chapter.py at row start. The
% manager agent (or a worker dispatched by it) fills the body below with the
% Lean-faithful definition statement(s). Stays close to the lecture notes.
%
% This file is a sibling of `main.tex` inside `<subsection>/tex/`. The
% `subfiles` package lets it compile both standalone (resolving its
% preamble via `main.tex`) and as part of `main.tex` via `\subfile{...}`.

\documentclass[main]{subfiles}

\begin{document}
% ref: def_3_8
% title: TopologicalOrder
\def\rowref{def\_3\_8}
\phantomsection\label{def_3_8}

\begin{Def}[Topological order]
    \label{def-topological-order}
    Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CDMG (def \ref{def-cdmg}); throughout we use the notation of def \ref{not-cdmg} and the family-relationship notation of def \ref{def_3_5}. By def \ref{def-cdmg}, items~i.--ii., the node set $J \cup V$ is finite (as a union of two finite sets).

    A \emph{topological order} of $G$ is a \emph{strict} total order $<$ on $J \cup V$ -- i.e.\ a binary relation $<$ on $J \cup V$ that is irreflexive, transitive, and satisfies trichotomy ($v < w$, $v = w$, or $w < v$ for every $v, w \in J \cup V$); the lecture notes' order symbol $<$ is read strictly throughout -- such that
    \[
        \forall\, v, w \in J \cup V :\quad v \in \Pa^G(w) \;\Longrightarrow\; v < w.
    \]
    The displayed implication is well-typed because, by the set-builder defining clause $\Pa^G(w) := \lC u \in J \cup V \,|\, (u, w) \in E \rC$ of def \ref{def_3_5}, item~i., any $v \in \Pa^G(w)$ automatically lies in $J \cup V$, so the comparison $v < w$ is defined.

    \emph{Equivalent indexing form.}
    Set $K := |J \cup V|$, the cardinality of the (finite) node set. Because $<$ is a strict total order on the finite set $J \cup V$, its order type is the finite ordinal $K$, so $<$ may equivalently be presented as a bijection $\{1, 2, \dots, K\} \to J \cup V$, $i \mapsto v_i$, with $v_1 < v_2 < \cdots < v_K$ under $<$. Under this presentation, the parents-precede-children condition above becomes
    \[
        \forall\, i, j \in \{1, 2, \dots, K\} :\quad v_i \in \Pa^G(v_j) \;\Longrightarrow\; i < j,
    \]
    i.e.\ \emph{parents always precede their children} in the enumeration $v_1, v_2, \dots, v_K$. The equivalence between the primary (strict-total-order) form and this indexed form relies essentially on the finiteness of $J \cup V$ guaranteed by def \ref{def-cdmg}: without finiteness, the indexed form would be strictly stronger than the primary form (it forces order type at most $\omega$).
\end{Def}

\end{document}
tex_statement.html:
Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CDMG (def def-cdmg); throughout we use the notation of def not-cdmg and the family-relationship notation of def def_3_5. By def def-cdmg, items i.–ii., the node set $J \cup V$ is finite (as a union of two finite sets).

A topological order of $G$ is a strict total order $<$ on $J \cup V$ – i.e.\ a binary relation $<$ on $J \cup V$ that is irreflexive, transitive, and satisfies trichotomy ($v < w$, $v = w$, or $w < v$ for every $v, w \in J \cup V$); the lecture notes' order symbol $<$ is read strictly throughout – such that \[ \forall\, v, w \in J \cup V :\quad v \in \Pa^G(w) \;\Longrightarrow\; v < w. \] The displayed implication is well-typed because, by the set-builder defining clause $\Pa^G(w) := \lC u \in J \cup V \,|\, (u, w) \in E \rC$ of def def_3_5, item i., any $v \in \Pa^G(w)$ automatically lies in $J \cup V$, so the comparison $v < w$ is defined.

Equivalent indexing form. Set $K := |J \cup V|$, the cardinality of the (finite) node set. Because $<$ is a strict total order on the finite set $J \cup V$, its order type is the finite ordinal $K$, so $<$ may equivalently be presented as a bijection $\{1, 2, \dots, K\} \to J \cup V$, $i \mapsto v_i$, with $v_1 < v_2 < \cdots < v_K$ under $<$. Under this presentation, the parents-precede-children condition above becomes \[ \forall\, i, j \in \{1, 2, \dots, K\} :\quad v_i \in \Pa^G(v_j) \;\Longrightarrow\; i < j, \] i.e.\ parents always precede their children in the enumeration $v_1, v_2, \dots, v_K$. The equivalence between the primary (strict-total-order) form and this indexed form relies essentially on the finiteness of $J \cup V$ guaranteed by def def-cdmg: without finiteness, the indexed form would be strictly stronger than the primary form (it forces order type at most $\omega$).

lean_blocks.explanation: This single line is statement-typing infrastructure rather than part of the LN definition itself. The `variable` command auto-binds `Node` (as an implicit `{Node : Type*}`) and the instance `[DecidableEq Node]` into every declaration that follows in this section, so we never have to repeat them on the `IsTopologicalOrder` signature below. The `Type*` annotation is universe-polymorphic — `Node` may live at any universe level, matching the convention set in `def_3_1`'s `CDMG`. The `[DecidableEq Node]` instance is load-bearing for this row in two distinct places: first, the `Membership Node (CDMG Node)` instance from `def_3_2` makes `v ∈ G` reduce to `Finset.mem` on `G.J ∪ G.V`, which needs decidable equality on `Node`; second, `G.Pa w` from `def_3_5` builds its set body around the same `Finset` membership predicate. Drop `DecidableEq Node` and the main statement below stops type-checking.

lean_blocks.code:
def IsTopologicalOrder (G : CDMG Node) (lt : Node → Node → Prop) : Prop :=
  (∀ v ∈ G, ¬ lt v v) ∧
  (∀ u ∈ G, ∀ v ∈ G, ∀ w ∈ G, lt u v → lt v w → lt u w) ∧
  (∀ v ∈ G, ∀ w ∈ G, lt v w ∨ v = w ∨ lt w v) ∧
  (∀ v w, v ∈ G.Pa w → lt v w)
lean_blocks.explanation: `IsTopologicalOrder` is a `Prop`-valued *property predicate* on a pair `(G, lt)`: it takes a CDMG `G` together with an external strict relation `lt : Node → Node → Prop` and asserts that this particular `lt` is a topological order of `G`. The body is a four-fold conjunction in LN order. The first three conjuncts make `lt` a strict total order on $J \cup V$: `∀ v ∈ G, ¬ lt v v` is irreflexivity ($v \not< v$); `∀ u v w ∈ G, lt u v → lt v w → lt u w` is transitivity; `∀ v w ∈ G, lt v w ∨ v = w ∨ lt w v` is trichotomy (Mathlib-aligned disjunct order, equality in the middle slot). The bound-variable form `∀ v ∈ G, …` is Mathlib sugar for `∀ v, v ∈ G → …`; the membership unfolds to `v ∈ G.J ∪ G.V` via `def_3_2`'s `Membership` instance. The fourth conjunct `∀ v w, v ∈ G.Pa w → lt v w` is parents-precede-children, and deliberately quantifies over *all* `v w : Node` rather than `v, w ∈ G` — membership is already baked into `def_3_5`'s `Pa` body, and `G.Pa w` is dot-notation for the `CDMG.Pa` operator there. The conjunction shape lets downstream consumers like `claim_3_2` destructure cleanly with `obtain ⟨hirrefl, htrans, htri, hpa⟩ := h`.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Property predicate: asserts external relation `lt` is a topological order on `G`.
def IsTopologicalOrder (G : CDMG Node) (lt : Node → Node → Prop) : Prop :=
  -- Irreflexivity: no node is strictly less than itself.
  (∀ v ∈ G, ¬ lt v v) ∧
  -- Transitivity of `lt` on the vertex set `J ∪ V`.
  (∀ u ∈ G, ∀ v ∈ G, ∀ w ∈ G, lt u v → lt v w → lt u w) ∧
  -- Trichotomy (Mathlib disjunct order: equality sits in the middle slot).
  (∀ v ∈ G, ∀ w ∈ G, lt v w ∨ v = w ∨ lt w v) ∧
  -- Parents precede children; `v ∈ G.Pa w` already entails `v ∈ G`.
  (∀ v w, v ∈ G.Pa w → lt v w)

design_choices:
**Typing context `Node : Type*` with `[DecidableEq Node]`, inherited from `def_3_1`.** Both fixtures are load-bearing here. The bounded quantifier `∀ v ∈ G, …` driving the first three conjuncts routes through the `Membership Node (CDMG Node)` instance from `def_3_2` (`CDMGNotation.lean`), which unfolds `v ∈ G` to `v ∈ G.J ∪ G.V` — a `Finset` membership check that reduces to decidable equality on `Node`. The fourth conjunct's `v ∈ G.Pa w` reaches back into `def_3_5`'s parent-set body `{u | u ∈ G ∧ (u, w) ∈ G.E}`, whose first conjunct is the same `Finset`-membership predicate and whose second tests pair membership in the `Finset (Node × Node)` edge set; both routes go through `DecidableEq Node`. Dropping either fixture would make the statement fail to type-check. Stronger instances (`Fintype`, `LinearOrder`) are not needed here and are deferred to consumers — `claim_3_2`'s constructive topological-sort proof may need them at the algorithmic step, but they would over-commit this row's interface.

**Predicate over an external ordering, not an existence claim or a fresh structure.** The LN reads "a topological order of `G` is a total order `<` of `J ∪ V` such that …" — it characterises *which* orders qualify, not whether one exists. Mirroring this, the Lean declaration is a `Prop`-valued predicate `IsTopologicalOrder : CDMG Node → (Node → Node → Prop) → Prop`. The downstream existence claim "an acyclic CDMG admits a topological order" will be stated by `claim_3_2` as `G.IsAcyclic ↔ ∃ lt, G.IsTopologicalOrder lt`; the existential is *not* baked into this row. A bundled `structure TopologicalOrder (G) where lt + props` shape was rejected because it would conflate the property this row defines with the witness data `claim_3_2` quantifies over, force every caller who just wants to *check* a candidate `lt` to package it first, and mislead downstream code into threading "the" canonical topological order through a graph when the LN never picks one — multiple topological orders generally exist and the theory does not privilege any single one.

**`lt : Node → Node → Prop` as an external argument, not a `[LT Node]` typeclass or a fixed canonical order on `Node`.** A `[LT Node]` instance would force *exactly one* canonical `<` per `Node` type, but a single `Node` type underlies every CDMG in the codebase and each CDMG may admit many topological orders; locking `<` to the type level would make `claim_3_2`'s existence claim either vacuous or unsatisfiable depending on which instance the user installs. A typeclass `[CDMG.TopologicalOrder G]` would face the same uniqueness pressure at the structure level. A canonical order baked into the definition (say, derived from a `LinearOrder Node`) would silently fix an ordering the LN deliberately leaves free. Taking `lt` as an explicit `Prop`-valued argument exposes the choice, leaves the existence question for `claim_3_2` to quantify over, and preserves the LN's property-vs-existence layering.

**Strict reading of `<`; the LN's "total order" terminology is unfolded to irreflexive + transitive + trichotomous.** The LN writes "a total order $<$" but uses the strict symbol $<$ rather than $\le$, so the order is read strictly throughout — confirmed by the canonical tex rewrite. The Lean encoding hard-codes the three strict-order axioms in their irreflexive form (`¬ lt v v`, not the strict shadow of a reflexive `≤`), and the LN's "for all $v, w \in G$" prefix is rendered uniformly as `∀ v ∈ G, ∀ w ∈ G, …` over all three clauses, matching the LN's literal quantifier scope. The trichotomy disjunct order `lt v w ∨ v = w ∨ lt w v` (equality in the middle) follows Mathlib's `Trichotomous` convention, easing any later lift to a subtype `Trichotomous` instance.

**Flat four-clause `∧` conjunction, not a Mathlib `IsStrictTotalOrder` instance plus an extra parent clause.** Mathlib's `Mathlib.Order.RelClasses` exposes `IsIrrefl`, `IsTrans`, `IsTrichotomous`, and the bundled `IsStrictTotalOrder` class, but they all assert their axioms on the *entire* ambient type. Ours must be restricted to `J ∪ V` — using the typeclass would either over-commit `lt` on nodes outside the carrier (a strictly stronger requirement than the LN, ruling out perfectly valid orders that leave non-nodes unrelated) or force a subtype wrapper `↥(G.J ∪ G.V)` with a derived relation that every consumer would have to coerce across. A custom helper `IsStrictTotalOrderOn (S : Finset Node) (lt) : Prop` was considered and rejected as premature scope creep. Unfolding the four conjuncts inline matches the LN's prose literally and gives downstream consumers one-line destructuring via `obtain ⟨hirrefl, htrans, htri, hpa⟩ := h`; `claim_3_2`'s proof will pattern-match this shape both ways. The conjunct order mirrors the LN's enumeration so the destructure reads in source order.

**`G.Pa w` via dot-notation through `def_3_5`'s namespaced parent operator, no `v ∈ G`/`w ∈ G` guard, no `v ≠ w` precondition.** The fourth conjunct spells the parent relation as `G.Pa w` rather than `Pa G w`: `def_3_5` defines `Pa` inside `namespace CDMG`, so `G.Pa w` reads naturally as "the parents of `w` in `G`" through Lean's dot-notation field resolution, matching the LN's $\Pa^G(w)$ shorthand letter-for-letter and keeping the chapter's parent vocabulary uniform. Spelling the implication directly as `(v, w) ∈ G.E → lt v w` would lose the `v ∈ G` witness baked into `Pa`'s body. The universal `∀ v w, v ∈ G.Pa w → lt v w` is well-typed without bounded prefixes because `def_3_5`'s `Pa` body already forces `v ∈ G`, and `def_3_1`'s edge-endpoint subset clause forces `w ∈ G` whenever `(v, w) ∈ G.E`; explicit guards would be redundant. Omitting a `v ≠ w` precondition is load-bearing: when `(v, v) ∈ G.E` (a directed self-loop), the implication forces `lt v v`, which contradicts irreflexivity. So the very existence of a topological order rules out directed self-loops — exactly what `claim_3_2`'s forward direction needs. The LN's "equivalent indexing form" $J \cup V = \{v_1, \dots, v_K\}$ with $v_1 < \dots < v_K$ is deliberately *not* formalised as a parallel predicate: finiteness of `J ∪ V` (from `def_3_1`'s `Finset`-valued `J, V`) makes it logically equivalent to the primary form, but introducing an `IsTopologicalOrderIndexed` companion would invite spurious case splits over which form is in scope and constructing the indexing bijection requires picking a specific enumeration the LN never picks. Any consumer wanting the enumeration form can derive it on demand as a separate theorem — likely via `Finset.toList` ordered by `lt`, or a `Fin K → Node` bijection — without polluting this row's API.
